name: Gulf of Tonkin
description: The Gulf of Tonkin incident refers to two separate events involving the U.S. Navy and the North Vietnamese Navy in August 1964. The official U.S. narrative claimed that North Vietnamese forces attacked the USS Maddox on August 2nd and the USS Maddox and USS Turner Joy on August 4th. This narrative served as the catalyst for the Gulf of Tonkin Resolution, granting President Lyndon B. Johnson broad authority to escalate U.S. involvement in the Vietnam War. Conspiracy theories and alternative historical interpretations surrounding the Gulf of Tonkin incident question the veracity of the official account, suggesting that the second attack on August 4th either never occurred or was deliberately provoked by the U.S. Proponents of these theories cite inconsistencies in the official reports, radar evidence, and communications logs. They argue that the Johnson administration exaggerated or fabricated the incident to justify a pre-planned escalation of the war. Mainstream historical scholarship acknowledges discrepancies and uncertainties surrounding the events of August 4th, including doubts about the accuracy of radar readings and the reliability of witness accounts. However, most historians maintain that while the Johnson administration may have used the incident opportunistically, there is no conclusive evidence to prove a deliberate fabrication of the attack. The debate revolves around whether the ambiguities are due to genuine confusion in a chaotic situation or evidence of a deliberate deception. Declassified documents have further fueled the controversy, revealing internal doubts within the U.S. government about the validity of the August 4th attack. The incident remains a subject of intense historical scrutiny and a significant case study in the relationship between government, public opinion, and the justification for military intervention.
subtitle: Alleged pretext for Vietnam War escalation

claims.claim: The second Gulf of Tonkin attack (August 4th) never happened.
claims.evidence: Conflicting radar readings, ambiguous sonar contacts, doubts expressed by some crew members, lack of physical evidence of an attack, declassified documents showing internal government skepticism.
claims.criticism: Mainstream historians acknowledge ambiguities but argue that these could be due to genuine confusion in a chaotic situation. Radar and sonar technology were less reliable at the time. Some witnesses maintained they experienced an attack.

claims.claim: The Johnson administration deliberately provoked the North Vietnamese to create a pretext for war.
claims.evidence: The USS Maddox was conducting DESOTO patrols, intelligence-gathering missions close to the North Vietnamese coast. The U.S. had been conducting covert operations against North Vietnam (Operation 34A). The swift passage of the Gulf of Tonkin Resolution suggests a pre-planned agenda.
claims.criticism: DESOTO patrols were routine intelligence gathering. While Operation 34A existed, there's no definitive proof it directly provoked the alleged attacks. The Johnson administration may have exploited the situation, but pre-planning is difficult to prove.

claims.claim: Key evidence was suppressed or manipulated to support the official narrative.
claims.evidence: Allegations of altered radar data, selective use of witness testimonies, and withholding of dissenting opinions within the government.
claims.criticism: While some evidence may have been interpreted in a way that supported the administration's narrative, proving deliberate manipulation is challenging. The fog of war and the pressure to present a unified front likely influenced the reporting.

keyFigures.name: Lyndon B. Johnson
keyFigures.role: U.S. President who used the incident to escalate the Vietnam War

keyFigures.name: Robert McNamara
keyFigures.role: U.S. Secretary of Defense who presented the official account to Congress

keyFigures.name: Edwin Moïse
keyFigures.role: Historian who has extensively researched and questioned the official narrative

keyFigures.name: H.R. McMaster
keyFigures.role: Historian who defends the official narrative in his book 'Dereliction of Duty'

mythologyConnections: The idea of a manufactured crisis to justify war echoes themes of deception and manipulation found in various mythologies.
The narrative surrounding the Gulf of Tonkin can be seen as a modern myth, shaping public perception of the Vietnam War.

timeline.event: USS Maddox reports being attacked by North Vietnamese torpedo boats.
USS Maddox and USS Turner Joy report a second attack (disputed).
U.S. Congress passes the Gulf of Tonkin Resolution.
Doubts about the August 4th incident begin to surface publicly.
Declassified documents further fuel the controversy and raise questions about the official account.

sources.author: Robert J. Hanyok

sources.title: Robert J. Hanyok

sources.title: Edwin E. Moïse
